items.author: AI Engineer
items.subtitle: YouTube
items.title: Your agent architecture has a half-life of 6 months — Dan Farrelly, CTO, Inngest
items.summary: 该演讲指出AI智能体架构迭代极快，建议开发者通过构建核心原语并将决策逻辑下沉至代码层，从而摆脱对单一工具链的依赖。通过采用持久化执行层，你可以构建出具备高灵活性和架构韧性的智能体，无需频繁重构即可适应技术演进。

items.author: AI Engineer
items.subtitle: YouTube
items.title: The Desktop Frontier — Ahmad Osman, Osmantic
items.summary: Ahmad Osman探讨了AI模型如何向高效、小型化发展，并预言在未来一年半内，个人消费级显卡（如RTX 5090）将能运行达到前沿级别性能的AI模型。该视频强调了拥有本地AI算力的重要性，主张通过软硬件优化实现个人对智能基础设施的掌控与主权。

items.author: AI Engineer
items.subtitle: YouTube
items.title: Through the AI Fog: The Architectural Decision Agentic Security Depends On — Manoj Nair, Snyk
items.summary: 由于AI自动生成代码会导致漏洞积压激增且存在数据泄露风险，单纯依赖概率模型本身无法实现有效的安全治理。演讲强调必须在AI工作流中引入确定的验证层，通过独立的校验机制来监控和纠正生成系统的产出，以应对AI带来的新型安全威胁。

items.author: AI Engineer
items.subtitle: YouTube
items.title: AI’s Jurassic Park Period — Aaron Stanley, dbt Labs
items.summary: 该演讲探讨了AI智能体为完成任务可能自主绕过系统限制的隐蔽风险，并将这一阶段比作AI的侏罗纪公园时期。演讲者建议通过设计可修正的机制、设立强制性的物理干预点以及在冲突发生时采取默认挂起并解释的策略，以确保AI的可控与安全。

items.author: AI Engineer
items.subtitle: YouTube
items.title: Security Track Intro — Randall Degges, Snyk
items.summary: 该演讲探讨了在AI开发时代，如何解决代码安全漏洞、自动化代理运行风险以及模型访问不稳定性等核心挑战。主讲人Randall Degges旨在通过分析这些瓶颈，探讨如何实现AI开发中的“默认安全”，帮助开发者在扩大AI使用规模的同时降低安全隐患。

items.author: AI Engineer
items.subtitle: YouTube
items.title: It's 10pm. Do You Know Where Your Agents Are? — Kim Maida, Keycard
items.summary: 该演讲探讨了AI智能体因权限过大而引发的安全隐患，并提出利用OAuth令牌交换技术（Token Exchange）解决此问题。通过为每次工具调用动态生成短期、最小化权限的令牌，确保智能体仅能执行符合安全策略的操作，从而有效防止越权破坏。

items.author: AI Engineer
items.subtitle: YouTube
items.title: Privacy-Preserving Intelligence — Steve Korshakov, Bee (acq. Amazon)
items.summary: 该演讲介绍了穿戴设备Bee如何通过端侧加密、远程证明和公开透明日志等技术，确保即使是被亚马逊收购后，公司内部及外部均无法触及用户的个人隐私数据。这套系统仅利用两万行内存安全代码构建，旨在通过严密的架构设计在AI智能化与极度隐私保护之间取得平衡。

items.author: AI Engineer
items.subtitle: YouTube
items.title: We Gave an Agent Production Code Access and Then Tried to Sleep at Night — Moritz Johner, Form3
items.summary: 本演讲探讨了如何安全地赋予AI编程代理生产环境访问权限，通过将代理权限拆分为确定性的执行层与受限的编辑层来降低风险。演讲者分享了利用微虚拟机隔离技术和严苛的安全架构，有效规避了提示词注入导致的供应链安全隐患。

items.author: AI Engineer
items.subtitle: YouTube
items.title: Your LLM Stack Is a 2008 Database With Better Marketing — Lovina Dmello, NVIDIA
items.summary: 这篇演讲指出大模型基础设施的安全漏洞主要源于配置错误而非模型本身，大多数生产环境因权限过大、网络扁平及密钥泄露等基础运维问题面临极高风险。演讲者建议应将机器学习基础设施视作传统IT架构进行安全管理，并优先选择低延迟的防护手段，而非盲目堆砌复杂的安全防御。

items.author: AI Engineer
items.subtitle: YouTube
items.title: Agentic Development Security — Ezra Tanzer, Snyk
items.summary: 本演讲探讨了AI Agent在拥有工具调用权限后带来的严重安全风险，并提出从生成内容、工具依赖及行为治理三个维度进行管控。演讲者介绍了Snyk通过异步钩子和本地监控工具，有效防范Agent越权操作与恶意风险的技术方案，适合关注AI开发安全与Agent治理的工程师观看。

items.author: AI Engineer
items.subtitle: YouTube
items.title: Agentic Security: Permissions, Provenance, and the Agent Supply Chain — Steve Yegge, Gas Town
items.summary: 史蒂夫·耶格（Steve Yegge）在演讲中指出，AI生成的代码不仅会扩大漏洞攻击面，还会引发“幻觉包投毒”等新型安全威胁。他强调必须将安全审查作为独立且贯穿始终的环节，并让AI代理配备专业的安全扫描工具来辅助自检，以应对自动化攻击风险加剧的迫切挑战。

items.author: AI Engineer
items.subtitle: YouTube
items.title: In the Land of AI Agents, the Verifiers Are King — Tariq Shaukat, Sonar
items.summary: 演讲指出AI智能体在处理复杂任务时，验证机制比生成能力更关键，以应对模型幻觉带来的错误。作者提出了“引导、验证、解决”的三阶段开发框架，强调高质量的代码库和严格的验证体系是企业利用AI提升生产力的核心竞争力。

items.author: @trq212
items.subtitle: Thariq · Claude Code @anthropicai. prev YC W20,…
items.text: if you've run into this, please restart Claude Code, fix should be propogating https://t.co/MJ49i4suEb

items.author: AI Engineer
items.subtitle: YouTube
items.title: Why Your Agent Disagrees With Itself (And What To Do About It) - Diane Lin, Datadog
items.summary: 该演讲探讨了如何处理大模型在执行任务时的不一致性，指出这种波动并非简单的模型故障，而是识别业务策略模糊区的关键信号。通过结合主动学习、语义记忆和情景记忆，你可以建立一套持续优化系统，在无需频繁微调的前提下，利用人类反馈解决模型分歧并提升决策的稳定性。

items.author: AI Engineer
items.subtitle: YouTube
items.title: Don't Let the LLM Drive - Ornella Bahidika & Joel Allou, Microsoft
items.summary: 该演讲通过构建AI语音辅导工具的实践经验，指出LLM不应掌控工作流的逻辑决策，而应由外部的状态机架构来约束和驱动流程。作者建议将流程拆分为严格的节点契约，通过外部控制逻辑确保AI仅承担内容生成的任务，从而解决智能体在复杂交互中表现不稳定的痛点。

items.author: AI Engineer
items.subtitle: YouTube
items.title: Your Voice Agent Doesn't Need a Frontier Model - Joel Allou & Ornella Bahidika, Microsoft
items.summary: 语音代理系统的核心竞争点在于极低的延迟，因此应优先选用速度快的小型模型，而非昂贵缓慢的前沿模型。通过确定性的架构设计来处理逻辑规划与记忆任务，让模型专注于自然对话，从而以低延迟实现高质量的交互体验。

items.text: I love using Claude Cowork to manage my calendar. Here's my prompt for it: Manage my calendar for the week. Requirements: 1. <20 hours of meetings 2. Dedupe conflicting meetings. Look at past weeks to see what kinds of meetings I decline. Dinners don't count against the 20 hours. Build a skill that you refine. Ask me before updating invites. What do you use Cowork for?

items.author: @levie
items.subtitle: Aaron Levie · ceo @box - your business lives…
items.text: In a world where there are strong open source alternatives that are only just behind the frontier models, you make yourself less secure and competitive by gatekeeping access to frontier model capabilities. If you play this out, even if America could fully ban access to open source models, other ecosystems wouldn't have that ban and they'd have a leg up on access to those models. They could use them against us -or just use them to better defend themselves- and our own companies wouldn't be able to keep up. The calculus of what you do about AI regulation simply must change to account for the fact that open weights models are far stronger than many expected.

items.author: @rauchg

items.text: Thankful to 🇦🇷 Argentina for once again giving it all. Grit, passion, talent, faith. To Messi for proving he’s the best ever. And to America 🇺🇸 for hosting such a wonderful event. Enhorabuena tíos 🇪🇸!

items.author: @levie
items.subtitle: Aaron Levie · ceo @box - your business lives…
items.text: A lot of people make the mistake of thinking that when AI costs drop, that spend on AI drops with it. Usually the opposite happens. When you make AI cheaper, it will get consumed more. Because now you can afford to use for a wider of tasks than you did before. You start to write more code. You review more code for bugs and security. You run agents on large data sets you couldn’t process before. And so on. Thus, for the foreseeable future, anything that lowers the cost of tokens will drive up inference demand. This also gives you some insight also into why even open source business models work in AI. No one is running these models on their devices; they’re running them in infra. Great time to be one of those providers.

items.author: @rauchg
items.subtitle: Guillermo Rauch · @vercel CEO
items.text: Incidentally, I’m very bullish on cybersecurity as one of the best benchmarks for superintelligence. The “IQ test” of software engineering. The best engineers I’ve worked with in my career have usually had a deep background or interest in security. It’s actually easy for a model to “one-shot an XYZ clone” and impress people on X. But that’s not a good test. Finding, patching, reversing, and exploiting require a cognitive skill that transcends programming languages, runtimes, frameworks… It demands true reasoning power from the model and “corner thinking”. Very, very few humans excel at this, let alone in ordinary day-to-day software writing. Seeing Kimi K3 do so well here bodes well for open models.

items.author: @levie
items.subtitle: Aaron Levie · ceo @box - your business lives…
items.text: Good post if you’re trying to understand AI diffusion. Progress driven by AI will ultimately be rate limited by its interaction with the real world. The reason coding, for instance, has been adopted so quickly is you can write a near infinite amount of code, test it, and run it -and it can add value- without anyone in the outside world ever having to do anything differently. A single person, from a computer, can just make something work end-to-end differently. This is not true for life sciences, where new drug development eventually needs to be tested for years. Doing a sale, which requires going back and forth with a prospect. Or even a contract, which has to be negotiated on the other side by your counterparty. “An AI will hand you a genuinely clever design for a jet turbine blade. It might be far more likely to work than anything your engineers came up with. It'll still probably fail, because that's the base rate at the edge of what anyone knows. The only way to find out is to build the blade and try to break it. That's the real limit on learning, and it doesn't care how smart you are. Coming up with ideas was never the hard part. The hard part is how fast reality answers them.” Incidentally, this is why you need an applied AI that actually takes intelligence and makes it useful within the workflows of existing industries. Model outputs -alone- are not enough in most cases. You need to actually change the underlying workflows, and you need systems to deal with the realities of the real world feedback loops that are in these industries. This is why there’s so much opportunity in the applied AI layer.

items.author: @zarazhangrui
items.subtitle: Zara Zhang · Builder. Dangerously skips permissions. Harvard’17. GitHub:…
items.text: We need to get used to the idea that code/software can now be disposable Some examples (all are discarded after usage): 1. Creating design playgrounds/modals for yourself to fine-tune the look and feel of a design 2. Creating HTML pages to help you understand the code 3. Spinning up a throwaway dashboard to inspect something once

items.author: @zarazhangrui
items.subtitle: Zara Zhang · Builder. Dangerously skips permissions. Harvard’17. GitHub:…
items.text: Whenever someone asks me "I'm just getting started with posting content on social media; what should I talk about?" My answer is: What are the top 3 questions that you get asked most frequently by friends/acquaintances/colleagues? Turn those answers into videos/posts. Just say it like how you said it to them in real life. That is your positioning. If you have repeatedly said something for >3 times, it's worth turning into a video One of my initial motivations for posting on social media was because I was tired of repeating myself in real life
